title: Merge two arrays
description: Merge two arrays and return a sorted merged array

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Create combined array = []
Step 2: Copy arr1 elements → combined = [1, 3, 5]
Step 3: Copy arr2 elements → combined = [1, 3, 5, 2, 4, 6]
Step 4: Sort combined array → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
Step 5: Return sorted array

Step 1: Create combined array = []
Step 2: Copy arr1 → combined = [10, 20]
Step 3: Copy arr2 → combined = [10, 20, 5, 15, 25]
Step 4: Sort combined → [5, 10, 15, 20, 25]
Step 5: Return sorted array

approaches.name: Brute Force O((n+m) log(n+m))
approaches.summary: Concatenate the two arrays into a new array and then sort it using a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort. This approach is straightforward and works for any two arrays but is not optimized for the fact that the input arrays are already sorted.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge Two Sorted Arrays Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution: concatenate two arrays and sort the result
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach to merging two sorted arrays. The intuition is straightforward: since we need a single sorted array containing all elements from both inputs, we can simply combine them into one collection and then sort that collection. This works because sorting algorithms can handle any collection of elements, regardless of their original arrangement. The key insight is that we don't need to leverage the fact that the input arrays are already sorted—we can treat this as a general sorting problem. The algorithm has three clear steps: 1) Create a new array with enough capacity to hold all elements from both input arrays. 2) Copy all elements from the first array into this new array. 3) Copy all elements from the second array into the new array (appending after the first array's elements). 4) Sort the combined array using a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort. The time complexity is O((n+m) log(n+m)) because we're sorting n+m elements, and standard comparison-based sorting algorithms have this complexity. The space complexity is O(n+m) since we create a new array of that size. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the sorting step—without an efficient sorting algorithm, this approach wouldn't work. Common mistakes include forgetting to allocate enough space for the combined array, incorrectly calculating array indices when copying, or using an inefficient sorting algorithm that degrades performance. The real code pattern involves array concatenation followed by a sort() call in most programming languages.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Create combined array = []
Step 2: Copy arr1 elements → combined = [1, 3, 5]
Step 3: Copy arr2 elements → combined = [1, 3, 5, 2, 4, 6]
Step 4: Sort combined array → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
Step 5: Return sorted array

Step 1: Create combined array = []
Step 2: Copy arr1 → combined = [10, 20]
Step 3: Copy arr2 → combined = [10, 20, 5, 15, 25]
Step 4: Sort combined → [5, 10, 15, 20, 25]
Step 5: Return sorted array

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sorting a Combined Array
ladders.desc: Implement a sorting algorithm to sort an unsorted array
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function in the brute force approach: the sorting algorithm that organizes the combined array. The intuition is that we need to arrange elements in non-decreasing order, which requires comparing elements and swapping them when they're out of order. We can use any standard comparison-based sorting algorithm like quicksort, mergesort, or heapsort. For this explanation, we'll use quicksort as it's commonly available in standard libraries. The algorithm works by selecting a pivot element, partitioning the array so that all elements less than the pivot come before it and all elements greater come after it, then recursively sorting the sub-arrays. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) If the array has 0 or 1 elements, it's already sorted (base case). 2) Select a pivot element (commonly the last element). 3) Partition the array around the pivot. 4) Recursively sort the left partition (elements < pivot). 5) Recursively sort the right partition (elements > pivot). Time complexity is O(k log k) where k is the array size, and space complexity is O(log k) for the recursion stack. This connects to the full problem (L0) because without this sorting step, we couldn't produce the final sorted merged array. Common mistakes include incorrect pivot selection leading to worst-case O(k²) performance, forgetting the base case causing infinite recursion, or incorrect partition logic. The real code pattern involves recursive calls with partition indices.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array size 4 > 1, proceed
Step 2: Select pivot = 2 (last element)
Step 3: Partition: [1, 3, 4, 2] → after partition: [1, 2, 4, 3] (pivot at index 1)
Step 4: Recursively sort left [1] → already sorted
Step 5: Recursively sort right [4, 3]
   Sub-step: Select pivot = 3, partition: [3, 4]
   Sub-step: Sort left [3], right [4]
Step 6: Combined: [1, 2, 3, 4]

Step 1: Array size 5 > 1
Step 2: Pivot = 9
Step 3: Partition: [5, 2, 8, 1, 9] → [5, 2, 8, 1, 9] (pivot at end)
Step 4: Sort left [5, 2, 8, 1]
   Sub-step: Pivot = 1, partition: [1, 5, 2, 8]
   Sub-step: Sort left [1], right [5, 2, 8]
      Sub-sub: Pivot = 8, partition: [5, 2, 8]
      Sub-sub: Sort left [5, 2]
         Pivot = 2, partition: [2, 5]
      Combine: [2, 5, 8]
Step 5: Combine all: [1, 2, 5, 8, 9]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Array Concatenation for Merging
ladders.desc: Combine two arrays into a single larger array
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of the brute force approach: concatenating two arrays into one. The intuition is simple—to process all elements together, we need them in one data structure. By putting both arrays' elements into a single array, we create a unified collection that can then be sorted. The algorithm involves: 1) Calculate the total size needed (n + m). 2) Create a new array of that size. 3) Copy all elements from the first array to the beginning of the new array. 4) Copy all elements from the second array immediately after the first array's elements. The key insight is that we're essentially performing a 'merge' by physical combination rather than logical comparison—we're putting all elements together without regard to order, letting the subsequent sort handle ordering. Time complexity is O(n+m) for the copying operations, and space complexity is O(n+m) for the new array. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) because the concatenated array becomes the input to the sorting algorithm. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in index calculations, not allocating enough space, or modifying the original arrays unintentionally. The real code pattern typically uses array copying functions or simple loops.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Total size = 2 + 2 = 4
Step 2: Create new array of size 4
Step 3: Copy arr1[0]=1 to index 0 → [1, _, _, _]
Step 4: Copy arr1[1]=2 to index 1 → [1, 2, _, _]
Step 5: Copy arr2[0]=3 to index 2 → [1, 2, 3, _]
Step 6: Copy arr2[1]=4 to index 3 → [1, 2, 3, 4]

Step 1: Total size = 1 + 2 = 3
Step 2: Create new array of size 3
Step 3: Copy arr1[0]=10 to index 0 → [10, _, _]
Step 4: Copy arr2[0]=20 to index 1 → [10, 20, _]
Step 5: Copy arr2[1]=30 to index 2 → [10, 20, 30]

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Copying and Index Management
ladders.desc: Copy elements from one array to another with correct indices
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves copying elements between arrays while managing indices correctly. The intuition is that when combining arrays, we need to place elements in specific positions in the target array. This requires understanding array indexing and sequential copying. The algorithm for copying one array to another: 1) Determine the starting index in the target array. 2) For each element in the source array: a) Copy the element to the target array at the current index. b) Increment the index. The key insight is that array indices are zero-based and contiguous—we need to track where we are in the target array to avoid overwriting or leaving gaps. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements being copied, and space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the arrays themselves. This connects to the core logic (L2) because concatenating two arrays requires copying both arrays with correct index offsets. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, forgetting to increment indices, or confusing source and target arrays. The real code pattern typically uses a for-loop with an index variable.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize target = [_, _, _, _, _], index = 1
Step 2: Copy source[0]=10 to target[1] → [_, 10, _, _, _], index=2
Step 3: Copy source[1]=20 to target[2] → [_, 10, 20, _, _], index=3
Step 4: Copy source[2]=30 to target[3] → [_, 10, 20, 30, _], index=4
Step 5: Done

Step 1: target = [_, _, _], index = 0
Step 2: Copy source[0]=5 to target[0] → [5, _, _], index=1
Step 3: Copy source[1]=15 to target[1] → [5, 15, _], index=2
Step 4: Done

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Assigning Array Elements
ladders.desc: Read from and write to specific positions in an array
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with individual array elements using indices. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we access them using numerical indices that represent positions. Reading an element means retrieving the value at a specific index, while assigning means placing a value at a specific index. The algorithm is straightforward: 1) For reading: value = array[index]. 2) For writing: array[index] = value. The key insight is that array indices typically start at 0 and go up to (size-1), so valid indices depend on the array's size. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations, and space complexity is O(1) additional space. This connects to the building block (L3) because copying arrays requires repeatedly accessing elements from the source and assigning them to the target. Common mistakes include using invalid indices (out of bounds), confusing zero-based with one-based indexing, or misunderstanding that assignment overwrites existing values. The real code pattern uses square brackets with indices.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has indices 0, 1, 2, 3
Step 2: array[2] refers to element at position 2 (third element)
Step 3: Value at position 2 is 30
Step 4: Return 30

Step 1: Original array: [5, 10, 15]
Step 2: array[1] = 99 means set element at index 1 to 99
Step 3: Replace value at index 1 (which was 10) with 99
Step 4: New array: [5, 99, 15]

testCases.input: Assign X to array[0] in [A, B, C]
Read array[4] from [a, b, c, d, e]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Ordered Collections
ladders.desc: Basic concept: arrays store multiple elements in sequence
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what an array is. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in a specific order. The intuition is that it's like a row of boxes where each box holds one item, and each box has a number (index) so we can refer to specific boxes. Key characteristics: 1) Ordered: Elements have positions (first, second, etc.). 2) Indexed: We access elements using numerical indices (usually starting at 0). 3) Fixed size (in many languages): The array has a predetermined number of slots. 4) Homogeneous (in typed languages): All elements are typically the same type. The algorithm for basic array usage involves: 1) Creating an array with a specific size. 2) Storing values in positions. 3) Retrieving values from positions. Time and space complexity aren't typically analyzed at this foundational level, but operations are generally O(1). This connects to the basic operation (L4) because accessing and assigning array elements requires understanding this foundation. Common mistakes include thinking arrays start at index 1, confusing arrays with other collections, or not understanding that size is fixed. The real code pattern involves declaration and initialization syntax.

examples.input: Create array of size 3 containing numbers 5, 10, 15
examples.trace:
Step 1: Allocate memory for 3 elements
Step 2: Set element at position 0 to 5
Step 3: Set element at position 1 to 10
Step 4: Set element at position 2 to 15
Step 5: Result is array with values [5, 10, 15]

examples.output: Array of size 3 with strings at indices 0,1,2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has 3 elements
Step 2: Index 0 contains 'apple'
Step 3: Index 1 contains 'banana'
Step 4: Index 2 contains 'cherry'
Step 5: Elements are ordered: first 'apple', then 'banana', then 'cherry'

What is index of first element in any array?
If array has size 3, what are valid indices?

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to traverse both sorted arrays simultaneously, comparing elements at each step and adding the smaller one to a new merged array. This method efficiently combines the arrays by taking advantage of their sorted order without additional sorting overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge Two Sorted Arrays Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete implementation merging two sorted arrays into one sorted array using the two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer approach efficiently merges two sorted arrays by leveraging their sorted nature. Intuition: Since both arrays are sorted, the smallest element in the merged array must be the smaller of the first elements of each array. By maintaining pointers at the current positions in each array, we can compare elements and add the smaller one to the result, advancing the corresponding pointer. This process continues until all elements from both arrays are processed, ensuring the merged array remains sorted without needing additional sorting. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize two pointers i and j to 0 for arrays arr1 and arr2. 2. Create an empty result array. 3. While i < len(arr1) AND j < len(arr2): Compare arr1[i] and arr2[j]. Append the smaller element to result and increment its pointer. 4. After the loop, append any remaining elements from arr1 (if i < len(arr1)) or arr2 (if j < len(arr2)) to result. 5. Return result. Time complexity: O(n+m) where n and m are lengths of arr1 and arr2, as each element is processed once. Space complexity: O(n+m) for the result array. Connection to L1: This builds on the key subroutine of comparing and selecting elements. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle remaining elements after the main loop, or incorrectly comparing pointers leading to index errors. Real code pattern: def merge_sorted(arr1, arr2): i, j = 0, 0; result = []; while i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: result.append(arr1[i]); i += 1 else: result.append(arr2[j]); j += 1; result.extend(arr1[i:]); result.extend(arr2[j:]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Compare arr1[0]=1 and arr2[0]=2. 1 <= 2, append 1, i=1, result=[1]. Step 2: i=1, j=0. Compare arr1[1]=3 and arr2[0]=2. 2 <= 3, append 2, j=1, result=[1,2]. Step 3: i=1, j=1. Compare arr1[1]=3 and arr2[1]=4. 3 <= 4, append 3, i=2, result=[1,2,3]. Step 4: i=2, j=1. Compare arr1[2]=5 and arr2[1]=4. 4 <= 5, append 4, j=2, result=[1,2,3,4]. Step 5: i=2, j=2. Compare arr1[2]=5 and arr2[2]=6. 5 <= 6, append 5, i=3, result=[1,2,3,4,5]. Step 6: i=3 (end of arr1), j=2. Append remaining arr2 from index 2: [6], result=[1,2,3,4,5,6]. Return result.

Step 1: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Compare arr1[0]=10 and arr2[0]=5. 5 <= 10, append 5, j=1, result=[5]. Step 2: i=0, j=1. Compare arr1[0]=10 and arr2[1]=15. 10 <= 15, append 10, i=1, result=[5,10]. Step 3: i=1, j=1. Compare arr1[1]=20 and arr2[1]=15. 15 <= 20, append 15, j=2, result=[5,10,15]. Step 4: i=1, j=2. Compare arr1[1]=20 and arr2[2]=25. 20 <= 25, append 20, i=2, result=[5,10,15,20]. Step 5: i=2 (end of arr1), j=2. Append remaining arr2 from index 2: [25], result=[5,10,15,20,25]. Return result.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare and Select Elements from Two Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that compares elements from two sorted arrays and selects the smaller one.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pointer merge. Intuition: When merging sorted arrays, at each step, the next smallest element in the merged result must come from the current fronts of either array. By comparing arr1[i] and arr2[j], we can deterministically pick the smaller element, ensuring the merged sequence stays sorted. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize pointers i=0, j=0. 2. While both pointers are within bounds: Compare arr1[i] and arr2[j]. If arr1[i] <= arr2[j], select arr1[i] and increment i; otherwise, select arr2[j] and increment j. 3. The loop stops when one array is exhausted. Time complexity: O(min(n,m)) in the worst case, as it processes until one array ends. Space complexity: O(1) aside from input, as no additional storage is used for the comparison logic. Connection to L0: This sub-routine handles the main merging logic; L0 adds handling of remaining elements. Common mistakes: Using strict inequality (<) instead of <=, which can cause instability or incorrect ordering with equal elements. Real code pattern: while i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: selected = arr1[i]; i += 1 else: selected = arr2[j]; j += 1.

examples.input: arr1 = [1, 3], arr2 = [2, 4], process until one array ends
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0. Compare arr1[0]=1 and arr2[0]=2. 1 <= 2, select 1, i=1. Step 2: i=1, j=0. Compare arr1[1]=3 and arr2[0]=2. 2 <= 3, select 2, j=1. Step 3: i=1, j=1. Compare arr1[1]=3 and arr2[1]=4. 3 <= 4, select 3, i=2. Now i=2 (end of arr1), loop stops. Selected sequence: [1, 2, 3].

examples.input: arr1 = [5, 10], arr2 = [1, 6], process until one array ends
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0. Compare arr1[0]=5 and arr2[0]=1. 1 <= 5, select 1, j=1. Step 2: i=0, j=1. Compare arr1[0]=5 and arr2[1]=6. 5 <= 6, select 5, i=1. Step 3: i=1, j=1. Compare arr1[1]=10 and arr2[1]=6. 6 <= 10, select 6, j=2. Now j=2 (end of arr2), loop stops. Selected sequence: [1, 5, 6].

testCases.input: arr1 = [1, 2], arr2 = [3, 4], select until one ends
arr1 = [3, 4], arr2 = [1, 2], select until one ends
arr1 = [1, 3, 5], arr2 = [2, 4], select until one ends
arr1 = [2, 4], arr2 = [1, 3, 5], select until one ends
arr1 = [10], arr2 = [5, 15], select until one ends
arr1 = [5, 15], arr2 = [10], select until one ends
arr1 = [0, 1], arr2 = [0, 2], select until one ends
arr1 = [100, 200], arr2 = [50, 150], select until one ends
arr1 = [], arr2 = [1, 2], select until one ends
arr1 = [1, 2], arr2 = [], select until one ends

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating with Two Pointers Over Arrays
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that traverses two arrays simultaneously using indices, comparing elements at each step.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the technique of using two pointers to traverse multiple data structures in sync. Intuition: Many algorithms require processing two sequences together; pointers (indices) allow us to track positions independently. By incrementing pointers based on conditions, we can efficiently navigate without restarting scans. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize two index variables, e.g., i and j, to 0. 2. Use a while loop that continues as long as both i and j are within their array bounds (i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2)). 3. Inside the loop, access arr1[i] and arr2[j] for comparison or other operations. 4. Based on a condition (e.g., which element is smaller), increment either i or j. 5. The loop terminates when one pointer goes out of bounds. Time complexity: O(min(n,m)) for the loop iterations. Space complexity: O(1) for the pointers. Connection to L1: This provides the traversal framework; L1 adds the specific comparison logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check bounds before accessing elements, leading to index errors. Real code pattern: i, j = 0, 0; while i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): # access arr1[i] and arr2[j]; if condition: i += 1 else: j += 1.

examples.input: arr1 = [1, 2, 3], arr2 = [4, 5], traverse until one ends
examples.output: Pointer positions: i=0,j=0; i=1,j=0; i=2,j=0; stop
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0. Both in bounds (i<3, j<2). Step 2: Suppose we increment i based on a condition: i=1, j=0. Step 3: Both in bounds (i<3, j<2). Increment i: i=2, j=0. Step 4: Both in bounds (i<3, j<2). Increment i: i=3, j=0. Now i=3 (out of bounds), loop stops. Pointer trail: (0,0)->(1,0)->(2,0)->stop.

examples.input: arr1 = [10], arr2 = [20, 30], traverse until one ends
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0. Both in bounds (i<1, j<2). Step 2: Increment i: i=1, j=0. Now i=1 (out of bounds), loop stops. Pointer trail: (0,0)->(1,0)->stop.

testCases.input: arr1 = [1, 2], arr2 = [3, 4], traverse until one ends
arr1 = [3, 4], arr2 = [1, 2], traverse until one ends
arr1 = [1, 3, 5], arr2 = [2, 4], traverse until one ends
arr1 = [2, 4], arr2 = [1, 3, 5], traverse until one ends
arr1 = [10], arr2 = [5, 15], traverse until one ends
arr1 = [5, 15], arr2 = [10], traverse until one ends
arr1 = [0, 0], arr2 = [0, 0], traverse until one ends
testCases.expected: i max=2, j max=0 or i max=0, j max=2

testCases.input: arr1 = [100], arr2 = [200], traverse until one ends
testCases.expected: i max=1, j max=0 or i max=0, j max=1

testCases.input: arr1 = [], arr2 = [1, 2], traverse until one ends
arr1 = [1, 2], arr2 = [], traverse until one ends

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Elements and Updating Pointers
ladders.desc: Practice comparing two values and conditionally incrementing an index based on the comparison.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves the fundamental operation of comparing two elements and using the result to update state (like a pointer). Intuition: In many algorithms, decisions are made by comparing data points, and actions (like moving a pointer) depend on the outcome. This builds logical flow and control. Step-by-step: 1. Have two values, a and b, from arrays or other sources. 2. Compare a and b using a relational operator (e.g., <=). 3. Based on the comparison, perform an action: if a <= b, increment the index for a; else, increment the index for b. 4. This updates which element is considered next. Time complexity: O(1) per comparison. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L2: This is the decision-making step inside the two-pointer loop. Common mistakes: Confusing the increment logic (e.g., incrementing the wrong index) or using incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: if a <= b: i += 1 else: j += 1.

examples.input: a=3, b=5, i=0, j=0; compare and update
examples.trace: Step 1: Compare a=3 and b=5. 3 <= 5 is true. Step 2: Since true, increment i: i becomes 1. j remains 0. Result: i=1, j=0.

examples.input: a=7, b=2, i=0, j=0; compare and update
examples.trace: Step 1: Compare a=7 and b=2. 7 <= 2 is false. Step 2: Since false, increment j: j becomes 1. i remains 0. Result: i=0, j=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Learn to retrieve elements from an array using integer indices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is key to working with arrays. Intuition: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has a position (index) starting from 0. By using an index, we can directly access any element, enabling random access and iteration. Step-by-step: 1. Have an array, e.g., arr = [10, 20, 30]. 2. Use an integer variable, e.g., i, to represent the index. 3. Access the element with arr[i]. For example, if i=0, arr[0] returns 10; if i=1, arr[1] returns 20. 4. Ensure the index is within bounds: 0 <= i < len(arr) to avoid errors. Time complexity: O(1) for access. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L3: This provides the values (a and b) for comparison. Common mistakes: Using an index out of range (e.g., i=3 for arr of length 3) or confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array arr has elements: index 0: 5, index 1: 10, index 2: 15. Step 2: Index i=1. Step 3: Access arr[1]: returns 10.
Step 1: Array arr: index 0: 100, index 1: 200, index 2: 300. Step 2: Index i=0. Step 3: Access arr[0]: returns 100.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to refer to positions.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for working with arrays. Intuition: An array is a collection of items stored in a specific order. Each item has a position called an index, which is a number starting from 0. Think of it like a row of boxes: the first box is index 0, the second is index 1, and so on. This allows us to organize data and access it efficiently. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array: e.g., arr = [10, 20, 30] means arr has three elements. 2. The indices are 0, 1, 2 for these elements. 3. To refer to an element, use the index: arr[0] is 10, arr[1] is 20, arr[2] is 30. 4. The length of the array is the number of elements, e.g., len(arr) = 3. Time complexity: Not applicable for this conceptual level. Space complexity: Not applicable. Connection to L4: This foundation enables accessing elements by index. Common mistakes: Thinking indices start at 1, or confusing the index with the element value. Real code pattern: In Python, lists are used as arrays: arr = [value1, value2, ...]; first_element = arr[0].

examples.trace: Step 1: The array has three elements: 5, 10, 15. Step 2: The first element is at position 0, so index 0 holds 5. Step 3: The second element is at position 1, so index 1 holds 10. Step 4: The third element is at position 2, so index 2 holds 15.
Step 1: Array has two elements: 100 and 200. Step 2: First element: index 0 is 100. Step 3: Second element: index 1 is 200.

approaches.name: Divide and Conquer O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Recursively divide the arrays into smaller subproblems, merge them in sorted order using a merge step similar to the merge sort algorithm. This approach is useful for educational purposes or when integrating with other divide-and-conquer algorithms.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge Two Arrays Using Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Complete solution merging two arrays into a sorted array using recursive divide-and-conquer approach
ladders.explanation: This approach treats merging two sorted arrays as a divide-and-conquer problem. The intuition is that we can recursively break down the arrays into smaller subproblems until we reach base cases (empty or single-element arrays), then combine the solutions in sorted order. This mirrors the merge step of merge sort but applied to two separate arrays. The algorithm works by: 1) Handling base cases where one or both arrays are empty, 2) Recursively dividing the arrays into halves, 3) Merging the sorted results from recursive calls. The key insight is that merging two sorted arrays can be decomposed into merging smaller sorted subarrays, then combining them. Time complexity is O(n+m) where n and m are array lengths, as each element is processed once. Space complexity is O(n+m) for the recursion stack and result storage. This approach is particularly educational as it demonstrates how divide-and-conquer can be applied even to problems that seem naturally iterative. Common mistakes include: not handling empty arrays properly, incorrect base cases, and inefficient array slicing that increases time complexity. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the merge subroutine as its core operation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Call merge_divide_conquer([1,3,5], [2,4,6])
Step 2: Both arrays non-empty, so divide arr1 into [1] and [3,5], arr2 into [2] and [4,6]
Step 3: Recursively call left halves: merge_divide_conquer([1], [2]) → returns [1,2]
Step 4: Recursively call right halves: merge_divide_conquer([3,5], [4,6]) → divides further
Step 5: merge_divide_conquer([3], [4]) → returns [3,4]
Step 6: merge_divide_conquer([5], [6]) → returns [5,6]
Step 7: Merge [3,4] and [5,6] → returns [3,4,5,6]
Step 8: Merge [1,2] and [3,4,5,6] → returns [1,2,3,4,5,6]
Step 9: Final result: [1,2,3,4,5,6]

Step 1: Call merge_divide_conquer([10,20], [15])
Step 2: Divide arr1 into [10] and [20], arr2 into [15] and []
Step 3: Recursively call left halves: merge_divide_conquer([10], [15]) → returns [10,15]
Step 4: Recursively call right halves: merge_divide_conquer([20], []) → base case returns [20]
Step 5: Merge [10,15] and [20] → compare 10 and 20 → add 10, compare 15 and 20 → add 15, add remaining 20
Step 6: Final result: [10,15,20]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Subarrays
ladders.desc: The merge function that combines two sorted arrays into one sorted array
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental merge operation that combines two already-sorted arrays into a single sorted array. The intuition is simple: since both arrays are sorted, the smallest overall element must be at the beginning of one of the arrays. We maintain two pointers (i for arr1, j for arr2) and compare elements at these positions. The smaller element gets added to the result, and we advance that pointer. This process continues until we exhaust one array, then we append the remaining elements from the other array. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty result array and pointers i=0, j=0, 2) While i < len(arr1) AND j < len(arr2): compare arr1[i] and arr2[j], append smaller to result and increment its pointer, 3) Append any remaining elements from arr1 (if i < len(arr1)), 4) Append any remaining elements from arr2 (if j < len(arr2)). Time complexity is O(n+m) as we process each element once. Space complexity is O(n+m) for the result array. This subroutine is the core of the divide-and-conquer approach at L0. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors with pointers, forgetting to handle remaining elements, and modifying input arrays. The connection to L2 is that this merge operation demonstrates the core logic of comparing and selecting elements in order.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result=[], i=0, j=0
Step 2: Compare arr1[0]=1 vs arr2[0]=2 → 1 smaller → result=[1], i=1
Step 3: Compare arr1[1]=3 vs arr2[0]=2 → 2 smaller → result=[1,2], j=1
Step 4: Compare arr1[1]=3 vs arr2[1]=4 → 3 smaller → result=[1,2,3], i=2
Step 5: Compare arr1[2]=5 vs arr2[1]=4 → 4 smaller → result=[1,2,3,4], j=2
Step 6: arr2 exhausted, append remaining arr1[2]=5 → result=[1,2,3,4,5]

Step 1: Initialize result=[], i=0, j=0
Step 2: Compare arr1[0]=10 vs arr2[0]=15 → 10 smaller → result=[10], i=1
Step 3: Compare arr1[1]=20 vs arr2[0]=15 → 15 smaller → result=[10,15], j=1
Step 4: Compare arr1[1]=20 vs arr2[1]=25 → 20 smaller → result=[10,15,20], i=2
Step 5: arr1 exhausted, append remaining arr2[1]=25 and arr2[2]=30 → result=[10,15,20,25,30]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing and Selecting Elements in Order
ladders.desc: The fundamental comparison logic that drives the merge operation
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core decision-making process: given two sorted arrays, how do we determine which element should come next in the merged result? The intuition is that at any point, the next smallest element in the combined set must be the smaller of the current front elements of the two arrays (since both arrays are sorted). We maintain the invariant that all elements before our current pointers have already been placed in correct sorted order. The algorithm: 1) Look at current elements arr1[i] and arr2[j], 2) If arr1[i] ≤ arr2[j], then arr1[i] is the next smallest (or equal smallest), 3) Otherwise, arr2[j] is the next smallest. This comparison is repeated until one array is exhausted. Time complexity for a single comparison is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the comparison operation itself. This logic is the heart of the merge subroutine at L1. Common mistakes include: using strict inequality incorrectly (should be ≤ to maintain stability), comparing beyond array bounds, and not handling equal elements properly. The connection to L3 is that this comparison logic relies on understanding array indexing and element access.

examples.input: Current state: arr1[i]=3, arr2[j]=2, i=1, j=0
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare arr1[1]=3 and arr2[0]=2
Step 2: Since 3 > 2, arr2[0]=2 is smaller
Step 3: Select arr2[0]=2 as next element
Step 4: Advance j to 1 for next comparison

examples.input: Current state: arr1[i]=5, arr2[j]=5, i=2, j=1
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare arr1[2]=5 and arr2[1]=5
Step 2: Since 5 ≤ 5 (using less than or equal)
Step 3: Select arr1[2]=5 as next element
Step 4: Advance i to 3 for next comparison

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in arrays using indices and understanding bounds
ladders.explanation: This fundamental concept involves accessing specific elements within an array using their positional indices. In most programming languages, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. The intuition is that an array is a contiguous block of elements, and each element has a unique numerical address (index) that allows direct access. To access an element: 1) Ensure the index is within valid bounds (0 ≤ index < array length), 2) Use the index to retrieve or modify the element. Time complexity for array access is typically O(1) as it involves simple memory address calculation. Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation. This building block is essential for the comparison logic at L2, which requires accessing arr1[i] and arr2[j]. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (using 1-based indexing instead of 0-based), accessing beyond array bounds (index ≥ length), and negative indexing confusion. The connection to L4 is that array indexing relies on understanding how to work with individual data elements.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has length 4, valid indices are 0,1,2,3
Step 2: Index 2 is within bounds (0 ≤ 2 < 4)
Step 3: Access element at position 2
Step 4: Counting from index 0: position 0=10, position 1=20, position 2=30
Step 5: Result is 30

Step 1: Array has length 3, valid indices are 0,1,2
Step 2: Index 0 is within bounds (0 ≤ 0 < 3)
Step 3: Access element at position 0
Step 4: Counting from index 0: position 0=5
Step 5: Result is 5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Working with Individual Data Elements
ladders.desc: Understanding and manipulating single values in a program
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves working with individual data elements - the basic units of information in programming. An element can be a number, character, or any simple data type. The intuition is that complex data structures (like arrays) are built from these individual elements. Basic operations include: 1) Storing a value in a variable, 2) Retrieving a value from a variable, 3) Comparing two values (using <, >, ≤, ≥, ==), 4) Performing arithmetic operations. Time and space complexity for these operations are typically O(1) as they involve simple processor operations. This basic operation is necessary for array indexing at L3, which requires working with individual elements. Common mistakes include: confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), type mismatches in comparisons, and not understanding value vs reference semantics. The connection to L5 is that these operations are applied to the primitive data types that form the foundation of all programming.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify the two values: 5 and 3
Step 2: Apply comparison operation: is 5 greater than 3?
Step 3: Evaluate: 5 > 3 is true
Step 4: Result: 5 is greater than 3

examples.input: Store and retrieve: Store 10 in variable x, then retrieve it
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create variable x
Step 2: Assign value 10 to x (x = 10)
Step 3: Variable x now contains value 10
Step 4: Retrieve value from x
Step 5: Result is 10

testCases.input: Store 25 in z, add 5 to z
Store 8 in a, store 12 in b, compare a and b

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Primitive Data Types
ladders.desc: Basic data types like integers and their properties
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept: understanding what data types are and how they represent information. Primitive data types are the basic building blocks of all programs. For this problem, we primarily work with integers. The intuition is that computers store everything as numbers, and different data types determine how those numbers are interpreted. Integers represent whole numbers without fractional parts. Key properties: 1) They have a specific range (depending on programming language), 2) They support arithmetic operations (+, -, *, /), 3) They can be compared (>, <, ==). Time and space complexity aren't typically discussed at this level as these are fundamental language features. This foundation is essential for all higher levels, particularly L4 which involves working with individual elements. Common beginner mistakes include: confusing integers with strings, not understanding integer division vs float division, and overflow errors with large numbers. The connection upward is that arrays at L3 are collections of these primitive data types.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Examine 42 - it's a whole number, no decimal point → integer
Step 2: Examine -7 - it's a negative whole number → integer
Step 3: Examine 0 - it's a whole number → integer
Step 4: All three values are integers

Step 1: Examine 3.5
Step 2: Check if it has a fractional part (.5)
Step 3: Since it has a fractional part, it's not a whole number
Step 4: Therefore, 3.5 is not an integer (it's a floating-point number)

testCases.expected: Yes (as a value, though type may differ)
